programmes.show_description: An egotistical saxophonist and a lounge singer embark upon a strained and rocky romance. Contains language which some may find offensive. [1977]
1/6. A devastating attack puts new boss of SO15, Rachel Carey, at the heart of a conspiracy. Contains strong language, some violence and some upsetting scenes.
Highlights of programmes on BBC Two.
9/15. Life coach Teresa hosts a wild walk in Kielder Forest. Will her traditional Northumbrian picnic and a-mazing activities find her in pole position by the end?
Charlotte Smith and Vick Hope are in the east of England following the journey of Britain's sugar beet, 100 years after the first production of home-grown sugar.
The latest news, sport, business and weather from the BBC's Breakfast team.
1/2. Ian Hamilton and guide dog Major head to Bathgate in West Lothian. The town has an incredible industrial past and is now a popular commuter area for Edinburgh.
9/29. It's judging day, and only one chef can go through to represent central England. Film producer Alison Owen helps decide the winner from the two remaining chefs.
Rival advertising executives do battle over a non-existent product. [1962]
Jan Morrow meets a handsome Texan, not realising he's the same person as philanderer Brad. [1959]
Antiques series. Paul Martin presents from Hopetoun House, near Edinburgh. Paul also discovers the architectural history of Hopetoun House.
Stephen Watson presents live coverage from the National Football Stadium at Windsor Park as Glentoran take on Linfield in the League Cup final.
6/20. Vernon Kay and unreliable tour guide Paul Gorton test Nitin Ganatra, Rev Kate Bottley and Gladiator Apollo on their knowledge of the UK. Today's tour is of Derry/Londonderry.
Ranger Donald witnesses the start of the deer-breeding season, Jane gathers the autumn harvest from the orchard, and Rosie gets plenty of satisfaction from a 2.5km open-water swim.
All the talking points and in-depth analysis from the final round of the 2026 Six Nations, featuring England v France, Ireland v Scotland and Wales v Italy.
3/12. Zara worries that Callum's accident has had a lasting effect, Emma and Rob call in the vet for a difficult birth, and Ally and Bethany set sail for an island sheep gather.
3/6. Under attack from intruders, the mums with young cubs must find the rest of the pride. Leopard Lediba risks it all for a meal, and the cheetahs face danger during sprint training.
Forensics must identify the attacker after a stabbed teen is left fighting for his life. Contains some strong language and upsetting scenes.
Singer-songwriter Niamh Regan creates new music with fiddler and composer Caoimhín Ó Raghallaigh.
Cellist, multi-instrumentalist, composer and singer-songwriter Naomi Berrill collaborates with singer-songwriter Molly O'Mahony.
Mark Carruthers looks at the political developments of the week and questions policymakers on the key issues.
2/7. The brilliant Ed Gamble introduces comedians Tom Davis and Judi Love to the stage. Contains some strong language and adult humour.
It's summer, and Mark is camping out in the hope of seeing some early morning bird activity, while Katie is searching for barn owl chicks.
In 2045, all adults have fled to the mainland, but three siblings are left stranded. Contains strong language, sexual violence and disturbing scenes. [2022]
5/6. Strange things are happening in Marvin Gardens, and they start to attract attention. Contains some strong language.
Fiona Bruce presents an hour of topical debate from Wythenshawe. On the panel are Lucy Powell, Harriett Baldwin, Kay Burley, Sian Berry and Fraser Nelson.
Highlights of programmes on BBC Two.
Once the stately home of Lord Manners, Avon Tyrrell in the New Forest is a fine example of an arts and crafts calendar house. Eric Knowles learns about its fascinating history.
7/14. The pitches are for an oat-powered snack range, a father and son's DIY gadget, real-world hide-and-seek adventures and a sun-shielding lip care range.
Mark Chapman presents highlights of the day's four Premier League games: Manchester United v Aston Villa, Liverpool v Tottenham Hotspur, Crystal Palace v Leeds and Nottingham Forest v Fulham.
Twenty-four hours a day, the latest national and international stories as they break.
The big political issues of the day.
16/30. Game show in which players score points by answering questions correctly while avoiding the Impossible answers.
16/45. Jason Manford hosts the quiz show where players face a question and pick from a board of six rankable answers. Some answers are good, but only one is Unbeatable.
1/25. In the ultimate knockout quiz, eight players do battle on the Tournament Run in a series of exciting tug-of-war quiz matches. Hosted by Alex Scott.
Dave Myers and Simon King look at baking bread. They make a classic brown loaf to go with a ploughman's lunch, and brown ale and cheese bread, served with onion soup.
How powered flight and photography changed how the world is understood. This episode explores how aerial photography has revealed Scotland's hidden ancient past.
6/26. This edition comes from Selby, North Yorkshire. Items of interest include an embroidery of Captain Oates by a private in his regiment, and a signed Beatrix Potter book.
37/76. Paul Martin is joined by experts Mark Stacey and Adam Partridge in the university city of Bangor. Among the items they value are a mirror and a boxed collection of toys.

programmes.show_name: New: Richard Osman's House of Games
programmes.show_description: 91/100. Former winners Steve Backshall, Steve Pemberton, Sam Quek and Rachel Riley test their general knowledge skills on this week's Richard Osman's House of Games: Champions.
16/20. Vernon Kay, with unreliable tour guide Paul Gorton, challenges Susan Calman, Max Fosh and Noreen Khan to separate the factual from the far-fetched as the quiz arrives in Dundee.
Bringing forensic journalism from all corners of the world.
3/6. Paul discovers a forgotten WWI German prison camp, remembers the navvies who built Europe's once-biggest dam, and crosses the moors to a kingly feast on an island in a loch.
26/31. Clive Myrie asks the questions in another semi-final. The specialist subjects are Jeff Buckley, the managerial career of Rafael Benítez, Andy Warhol and the classic Routemaster bus.
32/37. The quarter-finals continue with another elimination match. Both teams lost their first quarter-final and can't afford to lose again. Amol Rajan asks the questions.
The inside story of how social media algorithms sow division with real-world consequences. Contains some strong language.
Songwriter, vocalist and multi-instrumentalist Susan O'Neill collaborates with composer and improviser Phil Christie.
Insights and global interviews on the crucial news stories of the day. With Matt Chorley.   Followed by Weather.
6/6. The prophets have started to look unwell, and Michael still hasn't asked his questions. Contains some strong language.
2/16. Sandi Toksvig gets vocal with Ed Byrne, Jimmy Carr, Judi Love and Alan Davies. Contains adult humour.
